verses.commentary: Four kings are introduced. Historical names anchoring the narrative in international politics, a world of empires and warfare. This places Abram's story in the context of real geopolitical conflict.
Five kings of the Jordan plain face invasion. This is a coalition war. The cities of the plain, including Sodom and Gomorrah where Lot has settled, are caught in regional power struggles.
The battle site is specified. The Dead Sea region, later known for its desolation, becomes the theater of conflict.
Political context. Tributary states rebel after twelve years of subjugation. The thirteenth year brings uprising, a common pattern in ancient vassal relationships.

The eastern kings retaliate with a sweeping campaign. These are the original inhabitants, giant peoples later remembered in Israel's conquest narratives. The invaders demonstrate overwhelming military power.
Widespread campaign. From north to south, the eastern coalition conquers, showing their dominance over the entire region.
The invaders turn toward the south and west. Amalekites and Amorites, Israel's future enemies, are defeated. The narrative connects to later redemptive history.
The five kings prepare for battle in defensive posture, attempting to resist the overwhelming force approaching them.
Four kings against five. Outnumbered but the eastern coalition is more powerful, showing that numerical advantage doesn't guarantee victory.
Geographic detail affects the battle. Tar pits become death traps. The kings of Sodom and Gomorrah flee in total defeat, their choice of terrain working against them.
Plunder. The victors take everything. Economic devastation for the cities of the plain. War brings complete loss.

The personal crisis. Lot is captured. His choice to live near Sodom (13:12) now bears consequences. Abram's family is caught in international conflict, showing how spiritual compromise leads to temporal danger.

News reaches Abram the Hebrew, the first use of this term in Scripture, possibly meaning one from beyond the river or descendant of Eber. Abram has local allies, showing integration into the region while maintaining distinct identity.
Abram responds immediately. 318 trained men suggests a substantial household. Born in his household means lifelong servants, loyal and skilled. Family loyalty drives action even at great risk.
Night raid. Tactical brilliance. Abram defeats a coalition that had conquered the region. Complete pursuit far north shows determination and divine empowerment of the seemingly outmatched.
Total victory. Everything is recovered. Lot is rescued. The mission succeeds completely, reversing the devastation wrought by the superior force.
The king of Sodom meets Abram. The Valley of Shaveh becomes the setting for two crucial encounters that will test Abram's character after military triumph.

Mysterious figure. Melchizedek (my king is righteous) appears suddenly. Salem is likely Jerusalem. He is both king and priest, a unique combination. Bread and wine suggest a covenant meal. This enigmatic encounter precedes the more obvious temptation.

Melchizedek blesses Abram. God Most High (El Elyon) emphasizes God's supremacy. Creator of heaven and earth echoes Genesis 1:1. The blessing recognizes God's sovereignty, attributing victory to divine power.

Credit goes to God. The victory is attributed to divine intervention, not human strength. Abram gives a tenth, recognizing Melchizedek's priestly authority. This predates the Mosaic law. Hebrews 7 interprets Melchizedek as a type of Christ.
The king of Sodom makes an offer. Keep the plunder, return the people. This seems generous but is actually standard practice and self-serving, attempting to obligate Abram.
Abram refuses with a solemn oath. Raised hand signifies a vow. He uses Melchizedek's title for God, showing the encounter's impact on his understanding.
Total refusal. Not even the smallest item. The reason: Abram will not be indebted to Sodom. Only God enriches Abram. No compromise with wickedness, protecting both reputation and spiritual integrity.
Fairness to allies. Abram's men and allies deserve their portion. The refusal is personal, not imposed on others. Integrity doesn't require making others' choices for them.
